interactions.summary: The host asks whether Jiang experienced Canada as paradise, outsiderhood, or both.
Jiang says he suffered racial abuse in poor Canadian neighborhoods but also remembers the freer atmosphere of the 1980s and 1990s with gratitude.
The host agrees from his own childhood memories of unsupervised freedom.
Jiang extends the point into a critique of overmedicated, overcontained Western childhood that refuses the bodily reality of children.

claims.claim: Jiang says his childhood in Canada included serious racial abuse and ostracism tied to poverty, outsider status, and social mismatch.
Jiang says Canada in the 1980s and 1990s gave children more real freedom to explore, take risks, and fail than contemporary parenting culture allows.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative social diagnosis on 2026-03-05 about the 1980s-1990s.

claims.claim: Jiang criticizes Western child management for defaulting to drugging restless children rather than changing boring institutions or letting boys move physically.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang contrasts old childhood freedom with the image of modern parents literally keeping children on a leash.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Have your child on a leash, like literally a leash.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image condenses his critique of overcontrolled late-modern parenting.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang explicitly anchors the freer childhood comparison in the 1980s and 1990s, which matters for later chronology-sensitive comparisons about Western decline.
